does: This paper introduces TexOCR, a system for converting scientific PDF documents into compilable LaTeX source code, addressing the gap that existing OCR systems only target plain text or Markdown. The authors create TexOCR-Bench, a multi-dimensional benchmark evaluating transcription accuracy, structural faithfulness, and end-to-end compilability, and TexOCR-Train, a large-scale training corpus. They train a 2-billion-parameter model using supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards derived from LaTeX compilation tests that enforce both compilability and referential integrity constraints. The model achieves superior performance on their benchmark, and their analysis demonstrates that contemporary frontier models frequently fail to preserve critical document invariants like section structure consistency, correct float positioning, and valid label-reference links.
connects: This work extends the document understanding and OCR pipeline beyond previous efforts focused on plain text or Markdown extraction. It builds on established OCR architectures and document layout analysis while incorporating recent advances in large language models and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. The paper responds to limitations in existing systems that discard structural information critical for scientific communication. It relates to prior work on document parsing, math formula recognition, and structured text generation, while advancing beyond these by targeting the complete, compilable reconstruction of LaTeX documents. The use of RL with formal verification draws connections to recent work on using programmatic constraints and unit tests to guide model training toward reliable outputs.
enables: This work enables downstream applications requiring faithful digital preservation and machine-readable processing of scientific documents. It opens possibilities for automated document format conversion, enabling researchers to extract and repurpose scientific content while maintaining structural integrity and computational reproducibility. The verifiable reward framework could extend to other structured document formats and domains where compilation or formal validation is possible. The benchmark and training corpus establish infrastructure for advancing OCR in specialized, high-stakes domains beyond plain text. Future directions include scaling to multi-page document reconstruction, handling diverse document styles and languages, and applying similar verifiable reward approaches to other tasks requiring formal correctness guarantees, such as code generation and mathematical theorem proving.
